$comment: Concept -> spec router over br_tree/. keyword_index maps a BR keyword to the spec path(s) that document it; each spec record carries title, status, anchors, and lead summary. Regenerate with tools/gen_brtree_index.py.

specs.name: client-server
specs.title: Client-server
specs.summary: Deploying BR with a separated UI (client) and business logic/data (server) over TCP/IP. Configuration/deployment, not coding. OS-specific server setup is in platform; start-up directives in config-directives.

specs.name: config-directives
specs.title: BRConfig.sys directives
specs.summary: The start-up configuration file (BRConfig.sys) — holds environmental settings read by BR at launch (800 chars/line). Configuration, not coding. BR loads it at start-up, falling back to the legacyWBConfig.sys if BRConfig.sys is absent. Lines

specs.name: environment
specs.title: Environment variables & paths
specs.summary: How OS/session environment values feed BR and how filenames resolve to OS paths. The configuration directives that consume these are in config-directives; reading them at runtime is via ENV$.

specs.name: installation-tooling
specs.title: Installation & tooling
specs.summary: External tools around BR: source editors, the Lexi preprocessor, installers, runtimes, DLLs and diagnostic utilities used to deploy and support it. Setup, not coding. Most pieces ship from ftp.brulescorp.com (/Dll_Distr/…).

specs.name: platform
specs.title: Platforms & executables
specs.summary: The operating systems and executables BR runs on, how a session is launched, and the portability story. BR's promise is that the same program runs identically across platforms — platform differences live here in configuration, not in code.

specs.name: assignment
specs.title: Assignment & data movement
specs.summary: Putting values into variables and arrays and changing them in place: LET, the forced-assignment operator :=, MAT array operations, substring mutation, and the internal data table (DATA/READ/RESTORE). Operators that form the right-hand-side

specs.name: conditionals
specs.title: Conditionals (IF)
specs.summary: IF / THEN / ELSE / END IF branching. The conditions themselves — relational and logical operators, and the two meanings of = — are defined in expressions.

specs.name: data-types
specs.title: Data types
specs.summary: The literals and value kinds BR works with: numeric (integer / fixed-point / scientific) and string. Declaring a variable's storage (string max length, arrays) lives in declaration; the operators that combine these values live in expression

specs.name: declaration
specs.title: Declaration (variables & arrays)
specs.summary: Declaring storage: variable kinds and naming, DIM for string length and arrays, and runtime redimensioning with MAT. Putting values into variables/arrays (LET, MAT copy/arithmetic, READ/DATA) lives in assignment; the value kinds themselves

specs.name: expressions
specs.title: Expressions
specs.summary: Operators, precedence, and the numeric / string / relational / logical expressions that BR evaluates. Substring mutation and the LET form of assignment live in assignment; the IF / WHILE / UNTIL constructs that consume relational expression

specs.name: system-functions
specs.title: System (built-in) functions
specs.summary: BR's intrinsic functions — they return a value and are used inside expressions. Grouped below by purpose. User-defined functions (DEF/FN) are a separate topic in flow-control/functions-udf.

specs.name: error-handling
specs.title: Error handling
specs.summary: Trapping and recovering from runtime errors without halting. The numeric error codes themselves are the lookup table in 90-reference/error-codes; this spec is the mechanism.

specs.name: functions-udf
specs.title: User-defined functions (DEF / FN)
specs.summary: Defining your own FN… functions with DEF … FNEND, their parameters (by value vs. by reference), local scope, and return value. The library facility that loads and links functions across programs (LIBRARY statement, loading strategies, searc

specs.name: other-flow
specs.title: Loops & branching
specs.summary: Iteration and control transfer: FOR/NEXT, DO/LOOP, GOTO, GOSUB/RETURN, ON … GOTO/GOSUB, and EXIT. The IF decision itself is in conditionals; the conditions are in expressions. Targets are <line-ref>s (line number or label) — see syntax.

specs.name: syntax
specs.title: Lexical structure & program syntax
specs.summary: How BR source is written: line numbers, comments, identifiers, line labels, and multiple statements per line. The control statements that use line refs are in other-flow and conditionals.

specs.name: controls
specs.title: GUI controls
specs.summary: Graphical controls built on FIELDS: combo boxes, radio buttons, check boxes, buttons, text boxes, date pickers, grids/lists, native menus, plus field help. They share the FIELDS statement and attribute machinery in input-output and fields-a

specs.name: fields-attributes
specs.title: Field formats & attributes
specs.summary: The format and attributes parts of a FIELDS definition — what a field looks like and how it behaves. The statements that use them are in input-output. Reusable attribute/font names defined in BRConfig.sys live under 00-configuration/config-

specs.name: input-output
specs.title: Screen input/output (FIELDS)
specs.summary: Full-screen data entry: positioning text and input at any row/column with PRINT FIELDS, INPUT FIELDS, and RINPUT FIELDS. Field formats and attributes are in fields-attributes; GUI controls (buttons, grids, …) in controls; windows in windows

specs.name: windows-cursor
specs.title: Windows & cursor
specs.summary: Opening bordered windows (mini-screens) as DISPLAY channels, drawing borders, and locating the cursor. FIELDS I/O inside a window uses PRINT #w, FIELDS … — see input-output. This is the canonical home for the window OPEN spec; the printing

specs.name: database
specs.title: Database operations (SQL / ODBC)
specs.summary: This explains how a Business Rules! program can act as a SQL client — connecting to an external relational database (SQLite, MySQL, SQL Server, MS Access, …) through ODBC, submitting SQL statements, and reading result rows. BR treats a live

specs.name: file-model
specs.title: File model (types, access, sharing)
specs.summary: The concepts behind BR file I/O: file types, usage modes, access methods, file numbers, and multi-user sharing/locking. The statements that act on files are in statements; record layout is form-spec; keyed access is keys-indexes.

specs.name: form-spec
specs.title: FORM specifications
specs.summary: FORM describes the byte-level layout of a record (or a print line) — the field type codes that READ/WRITE/REWRITE and PRINT … USING decode/encode against. This is the canonical FORM reference; the file-I/O statements (statements) and the pr

specs.name: keys-indexes
specs.title: Keyed access & the index facility
specs.summary: Building and using index (key) files for fast record access by key value. The OPEN/READ/REWRITE statements are in statements; access-method concepts in file-model; record layout in form-spec.

specs.name: serial-comm
specs.title: Serial communications channels
specs.summary: Opening an RS-232 / serial port as a DISPLAY channel and reading/writing it with the ordinary file statements. The general OPEN/PRINT/LINPUT mechanics are in statements.

specs.name: statements
specs.title: File I/O statements
specs.summary: Opening internal files and reading/writing/updating records. Concepts (types, modes, access, locking) are in file-model; record layout in form-spec; keyed access (KEY=/SEARCH=) in keys-indexes. Display-file PRINT/INPUT/LINPUT and printer/se

specs.name: pcl-pdf
specs.title: PCL / Native Windows Printing escape codes
specs.summary: Rich printer formatting beyond plain PRINT USING: fonts, color, positioning, images, and boxes/shading — emitted as escape sequences (\E…) or PRINTER.SYS substitution tags ([NAME]) inside PRINT #255: output. The plain print statements are i

specs.name: sort
specs.title: SORT facility
specs.summary: Produces sorted output from an internal file via a sort control file of specifications. For ordered access without a separate sort, an index (30-io-file/keys-indexes) reads records in key order.

specs.name: statements
specs.title: Printing statements
specs.summary: PRINT (and PRINT USING) for screen/printer/display output, formatting with FORM/PIC, cursor positioning, page control, and opening printer files. On-screen PRINT FIELDS is in 20-io-screen/input-output; the record-layout FORM codes are canon

specs.name: fileio
specs.title: FileIO library
specs.summary: FileIO (a Sage AX library) is the standard file-access abstraction in this codebase: you describe each data file once in an ASCII layout file, and FileIO OPENs it, builds the FORM, sizes your record arrays, and defines FILE_FIELD subscript

specs.name: fnsnap
specs.title: FnSnap function packs
specs.summary: FnSnap is a shipped collection of ready-made FN… library functions (dialogs, screen helpers, printing/PCL, bar codes, dates, files, arrays, window maintenance). Link them with the library facility like any library function and call FNINIT o

specs.name: library-facility
specs.title: Library facility
specs.summary: Linking and loading reusable FN… functions across programs. Defining the functions (DEF/DEF LIBRARY, parameters, scope) is in 10-language/flow-control/functions-udf; shipped function packs are in fnsnap.

specs.name: screenio
specs.title: ScreenIO library
specs.summary: A Rapid-Application-Development library (a Sage AX tool) for building event-driven, screen-based applications; it builds on the FileIO library for data access. A Screen Function (built in the ScreenIO designer) is called like any library fu

specs.name: json-datastore
specs.title: JSON & data store
specs.summary: In-memory storage of named objects (JSON or other strings) plus functions to build, parse, and serve JSON — the data layer behind BR web services. Sending content to the browser is in web; the HTTP client OPEN is in 30-io-file/statements.

specs.name: web
specs.title: Web integration (BR as web server)
specs.summary: Running BR programs as the backend of a website: BR's own WEB_SERVER12.BR serves the pages, a page names a BR function via a ?BR_FUNC=… URL, and the WebForm library builds the HTML. A BR program can also reach out to web pages/services as a

specs.name: editing
specs.title: Program editing commands
specs.summary: Entering and editing program source in memory: auto line numbering, renumbering, deleting and listing lines. Loading/saving the program is in program-management. Each command has a short abbreviation (shown below).

specs.name: file-directory
specs.title: File & directory commands
specs.summary: OS-level file and directory operations, runnable interactively or from a procedure / EXECUTE. For opening files inside a program see 30-io-file/statements. Each command has a short abbreviation (in parentheses below). Options follow a leadi

specs.name: information
specs.title: Information & debugging commands
specs.summary: Inspecting state and debugging a running program: date/time, the command console, single-stepping, breakpoints, variable display, and logging.

specs.name: program-management
specs.title: Program management & procedures
specs.summary: Loading, saving, running, and chaining programs, plus procedure files (command scripts). Commands run immediately (no line number) and — crucially — from program code via EXECUTE. Line-editing commands are in editing; file/dir commands in f
